Negotiations are a two-way interaction. One of the biggest mistakes negotiator makers is going too fast. It's human nature to want to find the magic silver bullet, the quick and easy solution, especially if you are already averse to entering a negotiation in a first place. You want to get to your goal as soon as possible. However, this has negative consequences. If your counterpart feels as if they're not being heard, they'll feel exploited, pushed and marginalized. This undermines rapport and trust and consequently the strongest type of influence of all, which is trust-based influence. When negotiating and considering a negotiation strategy, people tend to focus all their energies on what to say or do. But a huge influence is how we are. Our general demeanor and delivery is both the easiest thing to enact and the most immediately effective mode of influence, sometimes as much as five times more influential. Our brains process more than the words. It also intercepts and reacts to the unspoken demeanor as well. Think about it this way. It's involuntary telepathy. Each of us is constantly signaling to the world our dominating feelings. These feelings are then being reacted to with what's known as mirror neurons in everybody's heads. We all have mirror neurons. This reaction is involuntary and immediate. It hits the animal mind, the limbic system we talked about earlier. If you go to work grumpy, people around you will feel uncomfortable and they don't want to talk to you. Mirror neurons. But if you show up at work and you radiate happiness and warmth, people will flock around you and want to strike up conversations. Mirror neurons. On the street, if you smile at someone and as a reflex, they smile back, mirror neurons at work again. Understanding this reflex of mirror neurons and putting it into practice is critical to the success of every negotiating skill you will learn. You have to be aware of how someone will perceive your tone and words. If they feel like you're being aggressive, they will be less likely to cooperate. And worse, in my old world, we ended up with dead hostages. In your world, you end up with a dead deal. So that's why we highlighted the three distinct voice tones and the two distinct inflections that are always available to negotiators. We've been talking about mirror neurons. Now we're going to talk about something that sounds similar but is completely different. Mirroring. So what is mirroring? How is mirroring used and how does it work? Now the hostage negotiator's mirroring is not the mirroring that you're used to. This is not having the same body language that they have, holding your hand to your chin. If they hold their hand to their chin, it's not doing any of the physical things or doing it the same or mirroring or mimicking at all. The hostage negotiator's mirroring and our mirroring is this, exactly this. It's repeating the last one to three words or the critical one to three words of what someone has just said. The intention behind it should be, please help me understand, but I need you to use different words. Mirrors always trigger people to re-explain and expand on what they just said with different words. It's as close to a Jedi mind trick as we have. It's insanely effective for gathering more information and getting someone to keep talking. Mirrors work magic. Now here are four simple steps for using and enhancing mirrors and they are as follows. First, use the late night FM DJ voice. Start with, I'm sorry. Mirror with an upward inflection and then go silent. Chat up, go silent for at least four seconds. If necessary, repeat.
